pages.raw_text:
A vastly different version than


that printed in the Spokesman-Re-


view of the riot" at Hillyard last


night is given The Press by men


who witnessed the proceedings and


feel that their town has been


slandered by deliberate misrepre-


sentation.


AS these witnesses tell it, the


trouble that resulted in the arrest


of A. Richardson was caused by


conductor No. 142, on a w. w. P.


Co. car, shaking and cutting nine
year old boy who hollered "scab." y


This boy is the son Of Engineer


Jack Croak, who was killed two
years ago.


Richardson protested against the
way the boy was treated and was
Immediately arrested by Specials


Byram and Hamlin. There was no
fighting or rock throwing, although
the railroad men were angry at the


rough treatment given a child of


their dead comrade by a grown


man. Five specials took part in the
arrest, and Richardson, who had
demurred at going on the car, was
beaten over the head with a billy
after he had been placed in seat.
Seeing this, George Banks warn
ed the specials to desist, as Rich
ardson was not resisting, and the
men would not see him abused.


Later when three of the specials


left the car they handcuffed Rich


ardson and he was brought to the
police station as if he were high


way robber.


On leaving the other specials at
Hillyard Byram caused laugh
by telling his colleagues to shoot


them down like dogs if they
had to-"


And then Deputy Sheriff McPugh
came along and took Byram's star,
telling him he had done enough
mischief for one time. McPugh
gave the railroad boys little quiet
talk and the trouble was all over,
as they had no intention Of Great
Ing riot. Somebody had placed
torpedoes on the track, but no harm
was done.


The Hillyard men who witnessed
the occurrence were ]. c. Patton
J. Lobner, w. Evans, S. s. Shields,


T. ]. Clark, C. Avery, George
, Banks, Lee Stout, Frank Cave and


r. y. Burbank. Shields is mer-


chant at Hillyard and the rest are
railroad men.
